text: Four robotic ports are positioned horizontally, two centimeters above the umbilicus, with an auxiliary port placed on the left side.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using the air seal system, inflating the abdominal cavity to facilitate robotic access.
The surgeon opens the lesser omentum and places a tissue tape around the hepatic pedicle for vascular control, with the procedure performed extra-abdominally.
A plastic tube is introduced into the abdominal cavity, followed by a clamping test to assess vascular control.
The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasonography to visualize and assess the future resection margins of the liver, using the robotic minimally invasive approach.
The surgeon opens the triangular ligament, revealing underlying anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon progressively mobilizes the left liver lobe, manipulating the tissue to achieve better exposure and access within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs proximal dissection at the level of the falciform ligament, focusing on the superior medial part of the left liver lobe.
The robotic arm number four retracts the left liver lobe medially, providing better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs progressive dissection of the left liver lobe, revealing the Arantius ligament as the dissection advances.
The surgeon marks the parenchymal transection line with a hook in the anterior-posterior part of the left liver lobe, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon performs intermittent pedicular clamping on the left liver lobe, applying the clamp for a period of ten minutes to control blood flow during the procedure.
The surgeon transects the liver parenchyma using cheliclasia, visibly separating the tissue along the marked transection line in the left liver lobe.
The assistant uses a Maryland bipolar forceps to perform irrigation in the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility of the area being operated on.
The surgeon uses vitalic clips to close small venous and portal branches leading to liver segment II.
The surgeon continues the parenchymal transection of the left liver lobe using cheliclasia, following the previously marked line.
The surgeon performs pedicular clamping, temporarily occluding blood flow to the liver to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon places a haemlock clip on a portal pedicle, securing the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic forceps to hold tissue while division is achieved with scissors, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon progressively mobilizes the specimen, detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues transsection in the proximal part using cheliclasia, electively dissecting the portal venous branches with precision.
The specimen is retracted to the left side, allowing the surgeon to continue the transection process using surgical instruments.
The specimen is retracted cranially and medially, adjusting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic forceps and scissors to divide the last remaining branches of the portal venous system, completing the dissection.
The assistant holds the suction system next to the bipolar forceps to prevent smoke from fogging the surgical camera view.
The surgeon uses a hook to coagulate the transection line, dividing the last parenchymal attachments to complete the dissection.
The surgeon places another Hem-o-lok clip on a vessel or duct within the surgical field.
An additional clip is placed on the specimen, followed by lateral traction to reposition it.
The surgeon places a final hemlock clip on the specimen, completing the clipping process.
The surgeon uses scissors to divide the remaining tissue attachments, completing the separation of the specimen.
The surgeon places the specimen into an ender bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon coagulates the transaction line using monopolar cautery, visibly sealing the tissue along the incision path.
The surgeon withdraws the tape used for vascular control, removing it from the surgical field.
The surgeon controls the transection line, ensuring hemostasis and stability of the tissue edges.
